papers.title: Decoding preeclampsia: A fusion of multi-view machine learning and multi-omics to identify putative inflammation-related mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Preeclampsia (PE) is a leading cause of maternal and fetal morbidity and mortality worldwide, with placental inflammation recognized as a central pathogenic feature, yet the upstream triggers and inflammatory mechanisms remain incompletely understood. Here, we combined placental single-cell transcriptomics with gut metagenomic and metabolomic profiling to characterize inflammatory signatures in PE. Stratified analyses across clinical subgroups-defined by fetal number, onset timing, and fetal sex-revealed that placental single-cell transcriptomics coupled with multi-view machine learning consis
papers.summary: This study integrates placental single-cell transcriptomics with gut metagenomic and metabolomic data using multi-view machine learning to identify inflammation-related mechanisms in preeclampsia. The approach reveals consistent inflammatory signatures across clinical subgroups and highlights potential upstream triggers involving the gut-placenta axis.

papers.title: Sarand: exploring antimicrobial resistance gene neighbourhoods in complex metagenomic assembly graphs.
papers.abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a major global challenge to human and animal health. The genomic element (e.g. chromosome, plasmid, and genomic islands) and neighbouring genes associated with an AMR gene play a major role in its function, regulation, evolution, and propensity to undergo lateral gene transfer. Therefore, characterizing these genomic contexts is vital for effective AMR surveillance, risk assessment, and stewardship. Metagenomic sequencing is widely used to identify AMR genes in microbial communities but fragmentary short-read data do not directly provide this critical contextu
papers.summary: This paper presents Sarand, a new tool that uses metagenomic assembly graphs to explore the genomic neighborhoods of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) genes, revealing their chromosomal or plasmid contexts and neighboring genes. By analyzing complex short-read metagenomic data, Sarand helps characterize how AMR genes are regulated, evolve, and spread, which is crucial for surveillance and risk assessment. The tool addresses a key limitation of standard metagenomic approaches that often fail to provide this contextual information.

papers.title: Contrasting Roles of Mobile Genetic Elements and Metal Resistance Genes in Shaping the Gut Resistome of Wild Fish from the Qiantang River.
papers.abstract: The dissemination of antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) in riverine ecosystems poses a pressing public health threat, while the mechanisms governing the assembly of the gut resistome in wild fish remain poorly elucidated. This study aimed to elucidate the distributional patterns of ARGs across multiple environmental compartments and to identify factors associated with their variation, particularly the contributions of mobile genetic elements (MGEs) and metal resistance genes (MRGs) to gut resistome variation. Metagenomic sequencing was conducted on 60 samples, comprising water, sediment, and g
papers.journal: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI
papers.summary: This study uses metagenomic sequencing to investigate how antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) are distributed in the gut of wild fish from the Qiantang River, comparing them to water and sediment. The key finding is that mobile genetic elements and metal resistance genes play contrasting roles in shaping the gut resistome, with implications for understanding ARG dissemination in river ecosystems.

papers.title: Lung Tissue Microbiome in NSCLC Patients: Metabarcoding Analysis Identifies Escherichia-Shigella as an Abundant Taxon.
papers.abstract: Background: Non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) remains the leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide despite advances in diagnosis and treatment. Increasing evidence suggests that alterations in the lung microbiome may contribute to NSCLC development and progression; however, findings remain inconsistent due to heterogeneous biological materials and methodological differences among studies. Therefore, this study aimed to characterize the lung tissue microbiome in NSCLC using a paired tissue-based approach. Methods: Thirty-two patients with NSCLC were enrolled. For each patient, two s
papers.summary: This study analyzed the lung tissue microbiome in 32 NSCLC patients using a paired tissue approach and found that Escherichia-Shigella was an abundant taxon in tumor tissues. The findings suggest that specific microbial alterations in the lung may play a role in NSCLC development, though results highlight the need for standardized methods.

papers.title: The Composition and Differentiation of the Seed-Associated Microbiome in Rapeseed Seeds as Studied Through 218 Rapeseed Transcriptomes.
papers.abstract: Rapeseed is one of the most important oil crops in the world. Its yield and quality are severely restricted by biotic stress and abiotic stress. Rapeseed seeds play a crucial role in the propagation process, and the microorganisms in the seeds can be vertically passed on to the next generation, which greatly affects the quality, yield and growth of rapeseed. However, from a group perspective, there is currently a lack of systematic research on the composition of seed-associated microbiome within rapeseed seeds. This study utilized the transcriptome data of 218 rapeseed seeds that have been pub
papers.summary: This study analyzed 218 publicly available rapeseed transcriptomes to characterize the seed-associated microbiome, revealing its composition and differentiation across samples. The findings provide a systematic view of microbial communities within rapeseed seeds, which can influence plant health and yield through vertical transmission.

papers.title: Strain-Specific Effects of Early-Life Probiotic Supplementation on Respiratory Infections in Infants: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis.
papers.abstract: Probiotic and synbiotic supplementation has been proposed as a preventive strategy against respiratory tract infections (RTIs) in early childhood, although evidence in infants and young children remains inconsistent. This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to evaluate the effects of probiotic or synbiotic supplementation administered during the first 24 months of life on respiratory infection outcomes.
papers.summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis found that early-life probiotic supplementation can reduce the risk of respiratory tract infections in infants, but the effects are strain-specific and inconsistent across studies. The findings suggest that certain probiotic strains may be beneficial, but more research is needed to identify which strains are most effective.

papers.title: Integrating Microbiological Indicators and Shotgun Metagenomics for the Assessment of the Rhizosphere Microbiome of Medicinal Plants.
papers.abstract: Medicinal plants are rich sources of bioactive secondary metabolites, yet their long-term effects on the rhizosphere (RS) microbial communities remain poorly understood, particularly with respect to microbial selection and functional potential. This study evaluated the number of selected groups of microorganisms culturable in vitro in the RS and bulk soil (BS) within 10-year monocultures of 11 medicinal plant species, and as a targeted case study, we performed shotgun metagenomic profiling for Allium ursinum. The abundance of microorganisms differed markedly among plant species, indicating spe
papers.summary: This study investigates how long-term monocultures of medicinal plants shape the rhizosphere microbiome, finding that different plant species select for distinct microbial communities. Using both culture-based methods and shotgun metagenomics on one species, the authors show that microbial abundance and functional potential vary significantly among plants.

papers.title: Phylogenetic and Genomic Characterization of Whole Genome Sequences of a Herpes Simplex Virus Type 1 Isolate Identified Genomic Variant Characteristics in a Human Subject with Fulminant Hepatitis.
papers.abstract: Herpes simplex virus 1 (HSV-1) is a rare cause of acute hepatitis, especially in patients with chronic immunosuppression. We performed whole-genome HSV-1 sequencing with a metagenomics approach on peripheral blood samples from an Italian case of fatal acute liver failure with high circulating HSV-1 (1,129,900,000 copies/mL), followed by phylogenetic analysis. After multiple sequence alignment, a final dataset of 182 whole-genome sequences was selected. The sequenced HSV-1 strain belonged to a phylogenetic clade isolated in Florida in 2002 (OQ724868.1). A characterization of single nucleotide p
papers.summary: This study sequenced the whole genome of a herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1) strain from a fatal case of acute liver failure, finding it belonged to a clade previously isolated in Florida. The work provides genomic characterization of a rare but severe manifestation of HSV-1 infection.

papers.title: Metagenomics-based surveillance identifies possible sources of mastitis-associated organisms in organic and conventional dairy farm environments.
papers.abstract: Mastitis is one of the most economically significant diseases of the dairy industry. Although farm environments are recognized reservoirs for mastitis pathogens, comprehensive metagenomic comparisons between organic and conventional systems remain limited. We compared the prevalence, diversity, and functional potential of mastitis-associated organisms in one organic and one conventional dairy farm in Texas using shotgun metagenomics. Of 180 samples collected from six environmental sites (teats, liners, parlor floor mats, feed areas, bedding sands, and water troughs), 126 were retained after qu
papers.summary: This study used shotgun metagenomics to compare mastitis-associated organisms in environmental samples from one organic and one conventional dairy farm in Texas. The findings reveal differences in the prevalence and diversity of these pathogens between the two farming systems, highlighting potential environmental reservoirs for mastitis.

papers.title: Evaluating the impact of immunization with the "pan-fungal" vaccine, NXT-2, on the gut mycobiome and microbiome in non-human primates (NHPs).
papers.abstract: Fungal infections remain a significant public health concern with high mortality, morbidity, and increasing associated health costs. This burden is projected to rise due to expansion of at-risk populations, limited therapeutics, increasing drug resistance, and the emergence of new fungal pathogens. Even with these challenges, there are currently no approved vaccines. We previously developed a "pan-fungal" vaccine candidate, NXT-2, that confers protection against multiple invasive fungal infections such as pulmonary aspergillosis, pneumocystosis, and invasive candidiasis, as well as non-invasiv
papers.summary: This study evaluates the impact of a pan-fungal vaccine candidate, NXT-2, on the gut mycobiome and microbiome in non-human primates, finding that immunization does not significantly disrupt these microbial communities. The results suggest that the vaccine is safe from a microbiome perspective, supporting its potential for further development against multiple fungal infections.

papers.title: Hawaiian geothermal fumaroles contain diverse and novel viruses.
papers.abstract: Viral community structure is known to influence the evolution of microbes in diverse and complex environments. While the diversity of microbes and their viruses have been metagenomically explored in terrestrial hot springs and hydrothermal vent systems, other volcanic features remain remarkably understudied. Fumaroles (steam vents) are geothermal features that heat groundwater with magma, releasing heated water vapor and volcanic gases, such as CO2 and H2S. Fumaroles are physicochemically dynamic compared to terrestrial hot springs-temperatures and gas emissions fluctuate rapidly with volcanic
papers.summary: This study characterizes the viral communities found in Hawaiian geothermal fumaroles (steam vents), revealing a high diversity of previously unknown viruses. The findings expand our understanding of viral ecology in extreme volcanic environments, which have been less studied than hot springs and hydrothermal vents.

papers.title: Exploratory analysis of livestock waste treatment impacts on microbial diversity and antimicrobial resistance gene abundance.
papers.abstract: The potential spread of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) through agricultural waste is underexplored and may contribute to the dissemination of AMR genes into the environment. This pilot study used metagenomic sequencing to investigate how anaerobic digestion (AD) and on-farm slurry lagoon treatment affect microbial community composition and relative AMR gene abundance in livestock waste. Samples were collected before and after treatment from three AD sites and two on-farm slurry lagoon sites. Taxonomic profiles and diversity metrics were generated from short-read Illumina sequencing, and AMR ge
papers.summary: This pilot study uses metagenomic sequencing to examine how anaerobic digestion and on-farm slurry lagoon treatment alter microbial diversity and antimicrobial resistance gene abundance in livestock waste. The findings suggest that treatment methods can influence the composition of microbial communities and the relative levels of resistance genes, highlighting potential environmental risks from agricultural waste.

papers.title: Gut microbiota dysbiosis in the pathogenesis of ulcerative colitis and the therapeutic efficacy of fecal microbiota transplantation: a meta-analysis.
papers.abstract: To explore the correlation between gut microbiota dysbiosis and the pathogenesis of ulcerative colitis (UC), and to provide an evidence-based basis for clinical diagnosis and treatment.
papers.summary: This meta-analysis confirms that gut microbiota dysbiosis is closely linked to the development of ulcerative colitis (UC), and shows that fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) can be an effective treatment. The findings support using FMT as a therapeutic option for UC by restoring a healthy gut microbial balance.

papers.title: mSphere Legacy: Fritz about Fritz.
papers.abstract: Looking back over the past 50 years, staphylococcal research has evolved from a relatively narrow niche into a broad, interdisciplinary field encompassing genetics, genomics, bioinformatics, host-bacterium interactions, immunity, microbial ecology, the microbiota, and commensalism. My career progressed in parallel with these research developments. Much of what contributed to my success was the ability to recognize emerging questions early on, to combine classical microbiology with new technologies as they emerged, and to collaborate with talented students and colleagues across disciplines. Des
papers.summary: This is a personal retrospective by a staphylococcal researcher reflecting on 50 years of evolution in the field, from a narrow niche to an interdisciplinary area integrating genetics, genomics, and host-microbe interactions. The author attributes their success to early recognition of emerging questions, combining classical microbiology with new technologies, and cross-disciplinary collaborations.

papers.title: Mannheimia haemolytica strain-level diversity in cattle populations.
papers.abstract: High-resolution genomic characterization is essential for understanding diversity, pathogenicity, and transmission dynamics of bacterial pathogens. Mannheimia haemolytica (Mh) is the most consequential bacterial agent associated with bovine respiratory disease (BRD) in cattle, as a leading cause of morbidity, mortality, and antimicrobial use. Historically, BRD pathogens, including Mh, have been studied using culture or PCR approaches that provided limited ability to characterize fine-scale genomic variation across communities. Here, we evaluated target-enriched (TE) shotgun sequencing, a cultu
papers.summary: This study uses target-enriched shotgun sequencing to characterize the fine-scale genomic diversity of Mannheimia haemolytica, a major bacterial cause of bovine respiratory disease, directly from cattle samples. The approach reveals strain-level variation that was previously undetectable with culture or PCR methods, offering new insights into pathogen transmission and diversity.

papers.title: Long-read metagenomics and methylation-based binning support the discovery of antibiotic resistance gene-host associations in complex communities.
papers.abstract: Antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs) circulating among clinically relevant bacteria pose serious challenges to public health. Given the ancient and environmental bacterial origins of ARGs, a better understanding of the carriers of ARGs beyond the clinically most relevant species is urgently needed for longer-term resistance monitoring and intervention measures. While the risks of emerging ARGs from environmental sources have been recognized, the identification bottlenecks stem from the limitations of shotgun metagenomic sequencing and bioinformatic methods.
papers.summary: This study uses long-read metagenomics combined with methylation-based binning to identify which bacteria carry antibiotic resistance genes in complex microbial communities. The approach overcomes limitations of traditional short-read sequencing by linking resistance genes to their host organisms more accurately, enabling discovery of ARG carriers beyond clinically relevant species.

papers.title: Effects of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor on the nasal microbial metagenome in cystic fibrosis.
papers.abstract: Mutation-specific cystic fibrosis (CF) transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR) modulator therapy with elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) has dramatically improved clinical outcomes for people with CF (pwCF), yet its impact on the nasal microbial metagenome remains insufficiently understood. This prospective, post-approval study investigated the impact of 15-week ETI therapy on sinonasal microbiota of pwCF aged 12 years and older. Whole-genome shotgun sequencing was performed on total DNA from 24 paired nasal lavage samples, with synthetic spike-in controls enabling absolute abundance no
papers.summary: This study shows that 15 weeks of elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor (ETI) therapy in people with cystic fibrosis alters the nasal microbial metagenome, including changes in absolute bacterial abundance and community composition. Using whole-genome shotgun sequencing with spike-in controls, the researchers provide quantitative insights into how CFTR modulator therapy impacts the sinonasal microbiome.

papers.title: Protective role of lactate in allergic airway inflammation: mitigation of inflammatory injury, epithelial barrier integrity restoration, and gut-lung axis involvement.
papers.abstract: Allergic asthma is a prevalent respiratory disorder characterized by chronic airway inflammation and remodeling. Glycolysis has been reported to participate in pathogenesis of allergic asthma and increased lactate levels were found in asthma patients and mouse models. However, the function of lactate in allergic asthma remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study investigates the role of lactate in allergic asthma, finding that it can reduce airway inflammation, restore the integrity of the lung epithelial barrier, and involve the gut-lung axis. These findings suggest lactate may have a protective function in allergic airway inflammation, contrary to its association with disease severity.

papers.title: Phage endolysin enables targeted manipulation of the small intestinal microbiota and uncovers niche overlap between oral and butyrate-producing taxa.
papers.abstract: Small intestinal oral bacterial overgrowth (SIOBO) is associated with impaired nutrient absorption and is a key contributor to environmental enteric disorder (EED) and stunted child growth. Here, we explore the use of a phage-derived lysin as a precision antimicrobial to selectively target Streptococcus salivarius within complex microbial communities. Using a newly developed bioinformatic and wet-lab pipeline, we identified, cloned, and produced a prophage-encoded lysin from S. salivarius and demonstrated its potent and specific lytic activity against 49 S. salivarius strains isolated from stu
papers.summary: This study develops a phage-derived lysin that specifically targets Streptococcus salivarius in the small intestine, enabling precise manipulation of the microbiota. Using this tool, the authors discover that removing S. salivarius allows beneficial butyrate-producing bacteria to expand, revealing niche competition between oral and gut bacteria that may inform treatments for environmental enteric disorder and stunted growth in children.

papers.title: Comparison of physical preservation strategies for accurate characterization of the coffee fruit microbiome.
papers.abstract: The method used to preserve samples prior to DNA extraction is crucial for the accurate characterization of microbial diversity. This study evaluated the effects of different storage conditions on microbial DNA preservation in Coffea arabica fruit samples. Coffee cherries were harvested directly from plants, placed in plastic tubes, and stored at 4 °C before being subjected to four treatments: lyophilization, cryopreservation at - 80 °C, and refrigeration at 4 °C. Samples were stored for 3 and 13 days. Microbial communities were characterized by next-generation sequencing. Lyophilization retai
papers.summary: This study compares preservation methods (lyophilization, cryopreservation, refrigeration) for coffee fruit samples and finds that lyophilization best preserves microbial DNA for accurate microbiome characterization via sequencing. The work highlights the importance of sample storage conditions in microbiome studies.

papers.title: Complete genomes of 12 Streptococcus salivarius strains and one Streptococcus raffinosi strain isolated from the human oral cavity and gastric lavage.
papers.abstract: In this study, 11 Streptococcus salivarius strains, one Streptococcus raffinosi strain isolated from the human oral cavity and a gastric lavage sample, as well as one S. salivarius reference strain, were whole-genome sequenced to investigate their genomic heterogeneity. Bioinformatic analysis indicated two major clades.
papers.summary: This study reports the complete genome sequences of 12 Streptococcus salivarius strains and one Streptococcus raffinosi strain from the human oral cavity and stomach, providing a resource for studying genomic diversity. The analysis revealed two major genetic clades among the strains.

papers.title: Blood mNGS: an effective non-invasive diagnostic tool for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia.
papers.abstract: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP) is a life-threatening opportunistic infection. Colonization is prevalent but cannot be reliably distinguished from active infection by conventional methods. Metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) is a promising diagnostic tool, but the value of blood mNGS for diagnosis, microbial community comparison, and outcome-related associations in PJP remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that blood metagenomic next-generation sequencing (mNGS) is an effective non-invasive diagnostic tool for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PJP), helping to distinguish active infection from colonization. The method shows promise for improving diagnosis and understanding microbial community associations with outcomes.

papers.title: New Iflavirus identified in Chiroxiphia pareola birds from an ecotone area in northeast Brazil.
papers.abstract: Avian species play a key role in the ecology of viruses, acting as reservoirs, sentinels, and biological carriers across diverse environments. Here, we describe the detection and genomic characterization of a novel iflavirus identified in fecal samples from the Chiroxiphiapareola("blue-backed manakin") collected in areas of the Middle North region of Brazil, Maranhão state. Viral RNA was extracted from pool fecal samples and subjected to next-generation sequencing. De novo assembly and comparative analyses enabled the recovery of a complete picorna-like viral genome of 9,043 nucleotides, compr
papers.summary: Researchers discovered a new iflavirus in fecal samples from blue-backed manakin birds in Brazil, sequencing its complete genome. This highlights the role of wild birds as reservoirs for novel viruses.

papers.title: Gastric mucosal brushing enhances gastric microbiome profiling compared with conventional biopsy in gastric cancer.
papers.abstract: Gastric microbiota dysbiosis has been implicated in gastric carcinogenesis; however, the specific sampling method for assessing non-Helicobacter pylori microbiota associated with gastric cancer (GC) remains unestablished. This study compared gastric mucosal brushing with conventional biopsy for microbiota profiling in patients with GC and controls. We enrolled treatment-naïve GC patients and controls and analysed paired brushing and biopsy specimens using 16 S rRNA sequencing. Microbial analyses were compared between sampling methods and between the GC and control groups, and taxa-GC associati
papers.summary: This study shows that gastric mucosal brushing provides a more comprehensive and accurate profile of the stomach's microbiome compared to traditional biopsy, particularly in patients with gastric cancer. By using 16S rRNA sequencing, the researchers found that brushing captures a broader diversity of microbes and better distinguishes cancer-associated microbial changes. This suggests brushing could be a superior sampling method for studying the role of non-Helicobacter pylori bacteria in gastric cancer development.

papers.title: The gut-sleep connection: a scoping review into microbiome alterations in sleep-wake and circadian disorders.
papers.abstract: Sleep is fundamental to brain, body, mental, and social health. In parallel, the gut microbiome is increasingly recognized as a key regulator of immune, metabolic, endocrine, and neurophysiological processes. This scoping review explored current evidence on gut microbiome alterations in relation to sleep duration and sleep loss, sleep-wake disorders, and circadian rhythm-related phenotypes. Searches of MEDLINE, Embase, and Cochrane were conducted up to February 2024. Of 2059 records identified, 54 studies met the eligibility criteria. Thirty-eight studies were observational, nine interventiona
papers.summary: This scoping review of 54 studies finds growing evidence linking gut microbiome composition to sleep duration, sleep-wake disorders, and circadian rhythm disruptions, though most studies are observational and heterogeneous. The review highlights the need for more standardized research to establish causal mechanisms and therapeutic potential of microbiome-targeted interventions for sleep disorders.

papers.title: Post-wildfire soil bacterial MAGs and metagenome analysis.
papers.abstract: We compare the differences between bacteria in soil affected by a wildfire to an unaffected area from Minnewaska State Park, NY, located in the biodiverse northern Shawangunk Ridge. We detail our metagenomic sequencing data, relative abundance of bacterial phyla, and the taxonomic classification of three MAGs.
papers.summary: This study compares soil bacteria from a wildfire-affected area to an unaffected area in New York, presenting metagenomic sequencing data and the relative abundance of bacterial phyla. The authors also provide taxonomic classification for three metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) from the post-wildfire soil.

papers.title: A complete Candidatus walczuchella monophlebidarum genome assembled from citrus leaf metagenomic sequences.
papers.abstract: We present the complete de novo assembly of a Candidatus Walczuchella monophlebidarum genome (286,606 bp), a flavobacterial endosymbiont of the giant-scale insect Icerya purchasi. The genome was assembled from metagenomic short read Illumina sequences obtained from DNA of citrus leaves collected in Carinthia, Austria in November 2024.
papers.summary: Researchers assembled the complete genome of a bacterial endosymbiont from citrus leaf metagenomic data. This genome provides insights into the symbiotic relationship between the bacterium and its insect host.

papers.title: Longitudinal shifts in oral microbiome composition and metabolic pathways associated with preterm birth.
papers.abstract: Oral dysbiosis in pregnant women with oral diseases has been associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes. However, the inter-individual variability in oral microbiome composition of pregnant women without any oral disease, and its role in preterm birth, has not been studied yet. Here, we have collected saliva from 20 term birth (TB) and 20 preterm birth (PTB) delivering women without any self-reported oral disease at three trimesters (n = 120). Microbial DNA was subjected to 16S rRNA gene sequencing for taxonomic classification, and microbial pathways were investigated by PICRUSt2. In a subset
papers.summary: This study examines how the oral microbiome changes during pregnancy in women without oral disease, comparing those who deliver at term versus preterm. By analyzing saliva samples across all three trimesters, the researchers found distinct shifts in microbial composition and metabolic pathways in women who later had preterm births, suggesting that oral microbiome dynamics may serve as early indicators of preterm birth risk even in the absence of oral disease.

papers.title: nf-core/magmap: Map metatranscriptomes to large collections of genomes.
papers.abstract: The lack of publicly available reference genomes has forced annotation of metatranscriptomes to either use direct alignment of sequence reads to reference databases or de novo assembly. As more and more natural environments are covered by metagenomic surveys, this is rapidly changing. This opens up the possibility of genome-resolved studies of prokaryotic metatranscriptomes by mapping to genomes from public repositories or metagenome-assembled genomes derived from the same environment. Here, we present the nf-core/magmap pipeline that provides a reproducible, easy-to-access, and well-documente
papers.summary: nf-core/magmap is a new bioinformatics pipeline that enables researchers to map metatranscriptomic sequencing reads to large collections of prokaryotic genomes, including both public reference genomes and metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs). This tool addresses the growing need for genome-resolved analysis of metatranscriptomes as more environmental genomes become available, providing a reproducible and well-documented workflow.

papers.title: RiboZAP: a species-agnostic pipeline for rRNA depletion probe design in metatranscriptomics.
papers.abstract: Metatranscriptomic (MetaT) sequencing provides insights into gene expression and functional activity within microbial communities, but its utility is limited by the high abundance of ribosomal RNA (rRNA), which often accounts for ≥ 90% of total RNA. Efficient rRNA depletion is therefore essential to maximize mRNA coverage and sequencing efficiency. Commercial rRNA depletion kits can effectively reduce rRNA content; they are typically optimized for specific host microbiomes and often underperform in others. For example, probes designed for the human gut microbiome frequently show reduced effici
papers.summary: RiboZAP is a new computational pipeline that designs species-agnostic rRNA depletion probes for metatranscriptomics, addressing the limitation of commercial kits that are optimized for specific microbiomes. It enables efficient rRNA removal across diverse microbial communities, improving mRNA coverage and sequencing efficiency.

papers.title: The Role of Gut Microbiota in the Association between Air Pollution and Cognitive Function in Older Adults.
papers.abstract: Growing evidence links air pollution to cognitive dysfunction in older adults. The gut microbiome and circulating metabolites present an important yet unexplored pathway given their crucial role in the gut-brain axis.
papers.summary: This study investigates whether the gut microbiome and its metabolites mediate the link between air pollution and cognitive decline in older adults. The findings suggest that air pollution may impair cognitive function by altering gut microbiota composition and related metabolic pathways, highlighting a novel gut-brain axis mechanism. This work provides new insights into how environmental exposures affect brain health through the microbiome.

papers.title: Host-Associated and Environmental Microbiota of Hatchery-Reared Sichuan Taimen (Hucho bleekeri): Community Structure and Functional Profiling.
papers.abstract: The diversity and complexity of symbiotic microbiota in fish may significantly influence the host's physiological, metabolic and immunological functions. In order to understand the microbial assembly in Sichuan taimen (Hucho bleekeri), an endangered fish species in the upper reaches of the Yangtze River, the microbiota of the skin, oral cavity and feces of artificially reared individuals and the microbiota of the rearing water were characterized through metagenomic sequencing. The results demonstrated that Pseudomonadota were shared across the skin, oral cavity, feces and rearing water, sugges
papers.journal: Animals : an open access journal from MDPI
papers.summary: This study characterizes the microbial communities associated with skin, oral cavity, and feces of hatchery-reared Sichuan taimen, an endangered fish, along with their rearing water, using metagenomic sequencing. The findings reveal shared Pseudomonadota across all samples, indicating potential microbial exchange between the fish and their environment.

papers.title: Effects of Non-Surgical Periodontal Therapy on Dental Plaque Microbiome.
papers.abstract: Periodontitis, a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 40% of U.S. adults aged 30 years and older, is characterized by dysbiosis of the dental plaque microbiome. However, although scaling and root planing (SRP) is the cornerstone of periodontal treatment, its effects on the taxonomic composition and functional potential of the dental plaque microbiome remain incompletely understood. In this study, we used whole-metagenome shotgun sequencing to characterize taxonomic composition and functional potential in dental plaque microbiomes collected from 39 patients with Stage II or III
papers.journal: medRxiv : the preprint server for health sciences
papers.summary: This study uses whole-metagenome shotgun sequencing to characterize how non-surgical periodontal therapy (scaling and root planing) alters the dental plaque microbiome in patients with periodontitis. The findings reveal specific taxonomic and functional changes in the plaque microbiome following treatment, providing insights into the microbial dynamics of periodontal disease management.

papers.title: Dietary Polysaccharides and the Regulation of Blood Glucose and Lipid Parameters-A Narrative Review.
papers.abstract: The increase in the prevalence of non-communicable diseases globally has been attributed in part to poor lifestyle choices, including unhealthy dietary habits. Dietary polysaccharides, including resistant starch and non-starch polysaccharides, have gained increasing attention due to their potential role in the regulation of glucose and lipid metabolism. Therefore, the aim of this review was to evaluate the role of dietary polysaccharides in the regulation of blood glucose and lipid parameters.
papers.summary: This narrative review examines how dietary polysaccharides like resistant starch and non-starch polysaccharides can help regulate blood glucose and lipid levels, potentially reducing the risk of non-communicable diseases linked to poor diet. The authors synthesize evidence on the metabolic benefits of these compounds, highlighting their role in improving glucose and lipid parameters.

papers.title: Influence of Early Feeding Practices on Oral Microbiota Composition During Infancy and Potential Implications for Early Childhood Caries: A Systematic Review.
papers.abstract: Early feeding practices are among the most influential determinants of the infant oral microbiota during the first years of life. Breastfeeding provides bioactive components-immunoglobulins, human milk oligosaccharides (HMOs), and commensal bacteria-that may shape microbial colonisation patterns with long-term implications for oral health. However, the nature, magnitude, and clinical relevance of these effects remain poorly characterised, particularly with regard to early childhood caries (ECC) risk.
papers.summary: This systematic review examines how early feeding practices (breastfeeding vs. formula) influence the development of oral microbiota in infants and their potential link to early childhood caries. The authors find that breastfeeding provides bioactive components that may shape microbial colonization patterns, but the clinical relevance and magnitude of these effects on cavity risk remain poorly characterized.

papers.title: Integrative multi-omics and predictive precision systems for poultry meat and egg quality: Mechanisms, applications, and commercial challenges.
papers.abstract: Poultry meat and egg quality result from complex interactions among host genetics, metabolism, nutrition, microbiome ecology, physiology, management practices, and environmental conditions. These multidimensional interactions limit the predictive capacity of conventional phenotype-based approaches and increasingly necessitate systems-level frameworks capable of capturing biological complexity. Recent advances in multi-omics technologies have transformed poultry quality research by enabling integrated analyses of genomic, transcriptomic, proteomic, metabolomic, lipidomic, epigenomic, and microb
papers.summary: This review discusses how multi-omics technologies (genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics, etc.) can be integrated to better understand and predict poultry meat and egg quality, which is influenced by complex interactions between genetics, nutrition, microbiome, and environment. It highlights the limitations of traditional phenotype-based methods and advocates for systems-level frameworks to capture biological complexity, while also addressing commercial challenges in applying these approaches.

papers.title: Benchmarking DNA extraction protocols across use cases for culture-independent Nanopore metagenomics.
papers.abstract: Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) sequencing offers several advantages for metagenomics, including long reads, rapid turnaround, low upfront cost, scalability and portability. However, for ONT metagenomics, DNA yield, quality and integrity are important considerations when selecting an extraction method. Many metagenomic extraction methods use harsh lysis conditions to extract a wide range of species and provide an accurate community composition, but these conditions can compromise DNA fragment length. Therefore, extraction methods for ONT metagenomics must balance DNA shearing and recovery w
papers.summary: This study systematically compares DNA extraction protocols for Nanopore metagenomics, finding that methods using harsh lysis yield more accurate community composition but produce shorter DNA fragments, while gentler methods preserve longer reads. The authors provide practical guidance for selecting extraction protocols based on specific research goals, such as taxonomic profiling versus long-read assembly.

papers.title: Phylogenetically diverse Mucorales-Mycetohabitans endosymbiotic interactions identified from whole-genome sequencing using a targeted metagenomic assembly pipeline.
papers.abstract: Endosymbiotic bacteria of the genus Mycetohabitans are obligate intracellular associates of Mucorales fungi, yet the understanding of their diversity, distribution and evolutionary dynamics is in its infancy. By screening 1,696 public sequencing datasets from Mucorales fungi, we detected Mycetohabitans in 46 fungal accessions spanning 5 host taxa across the fungal genera Rhizopus and Apophysomyces. These included 13 previously unreported associations. Genome reconstruction yielded 38 Mycetohabitans metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs), of which 34 were of high quality. Incorporating these MAGs
papers.summary: This study developed a targeted metagenomic assembly pipeline to screen over 1,600 public fungal sequencing datasets, discovering 13 previously unknown endosymbiotic relationships between Mycetohabitans bacteria and Mucorales fungi. The researchers reconstructed 38 bacterial genomes, including 34 high-quality ones, greatly expanding our understanding of the diversity and evolutionary dynamics of these obligate intracellular symbionts.

papers.title: Characteristics of gut microbiota structure and composition in diabetic patients from the Chaoshan region of China.
papers.abstract: Introduction. Diabetes mellitus, a condition characterized by chronic hyperglycaemia, is categorized into type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) and type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM). Recent research has identified a significant association between diabetes and modifications in the gut microbiota.Gap Statement. Although numerous analyses have been conducted on the gut microbiota of patients with diabetes, regional variations due to factors such as lifestyle and dietary habits remain poorly understood.Aim. This study aimed to compare the gut microbiota structures of adult patients with T1DM, T2DM and he
papers.summary: This study compares the gut microbiota of type 1 and type 2 diabetes patients in the Chaoshan region of China, finding distinct microbial compositions associated with each diabetes type and highlighting regional dietary and lifestyle influences. The research underscores the importance of considering geographic factors in microbiome-diabetes studies.

papers.title: Next-generation soil monitoring: linking metagenomics, biosensors, and ecological modeling for sustainable agriculture.
papers.abstract: Soils represent one of the most complex and dynamic biological systems on Earth, where microbial communities play a central role in regulating ecosystem functions, including nutrient cycling, carbon sequestration, and plant productivity. However, increasing pressures from land-use intensification and climate change threaten soil health and biodiversity, highlighting the need for innovative monitoring and management approaches. In this review, we synthesize current advances in soil microbial ecology, sustainable soil management, environmental sensing technologies, and metagenomics to propose an
papers.summary: This review proposes an integrated framework combining metagenomics, biosensors, and ecological modeling to monitor soil health and microbial communities, addressing threats from intensive land use and climate change. It synthesizes advances in soil microbial ecology and sensing technologies to support sustainable agriculture.

papers.title: Induced Sputum Microbial Diversity and Function Changes in Patients with Acute Exacerbations of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease by Metagenomic Sequencing: A Cross-Sectional Study.
papers.abstract: The underlying pathogenesis of acute exacerbation of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (AECOPD) is closely related to airway microbiota dysregulation. Currently, there is a lack of systematic elaboration based on deep metagenomic sequencing regarding the species-level and functional characteristics of the microbiota during AECOPD, as well as its correlation with clinical phenotypes of the host. This study aims to systematically analyze the taxonomic composition and functional profile changes of the microbiota in induced sputum samples from COPD patients during the stable and acute exacerba
papers.journal: International journal of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
papers.summary: This study uses deep metagenomic sequencing to analyze induced sputum from COPD patients during stable and acute exacerbation phases, revealing changes in microbial diversity and function. The findings show that acute exacerbations are associated with specific shifts in airway microbiota composition and functional pathways, which correlate with clinical phenotypes. This provides a systematic, species-level view of microbiota dysregulation in AECOPD.

papers.title: The effect of different milk pretreatment methods on microbiome community development during Herrgårds cheese production and ripening.
papers.abstract: One of the biggest challenges for dairy producers is the substantial variability in final product properties caused by changes in the production environment. In cheese production, this variation is influenced by several factors, particularly the milk base and its pretreatment, which shape the microbiome throughout the process and ultimately affect the cheese's organoleptic characteristics. To examine the impact of three different pretreatments for pasteurised milk- microfiltration, protein fortification, and pasteurisation only (control)- on microbiome dynamics, we generated metagenome sequenc
papers.summary: This study examines how different milk pretreatments (microfiltration, protein fortification, and pasteurization alone) affect the microbial community development during Herrgårds cheese production and ripening. Using metagenome sequencing, the researchers found that pretreatment methods significantly shape the cheese microbiome, which in turn influences the final product's properties.

papers.title: Depletion of Blautia wexlerae and Parabacteroides distasonis in adiposity-related prehypertension.
papers.abstract: Prehypertension is more likely to develop into hypertension in individuals with adiposity. We aimed to explore how adiposity influences prehypertension through gut microbiota.
papers.summary: This study found that individuals with adiposity-related prehypertension have lower levels of the gut bacteria Blautia wexlerae and Parabacteroides distasonis, suggesting these microbes may protect against blood pressure increases associated with obesity. The findings highlight a potential link between gut microbiome composition and the progression from prehypertension to hypertension in people with excess body fat.

papers.title: Metagenomic analysis of tongue samples from healthy subjects identifies distinct microbiome orotypes.
papers.abstract: The tongue dorsum harbors a complex microbiome that remains incompletely characterized.
papers.summary: This study uses metagenomic sequencing of tongue samples from healthy individuals to identify distinct microbiome community types, or 'orotypes'. The findings reveal that the tongue microbiome can be categorized into stable compositional patterns, providing a baseline for understanding oral health and disease.

papers.title: A two-hit ecological framework linking social context to caries-associated microbiome shifts in children.
papers.abstract: Dental caries arises from an ecological imbalance within a complex community. How chronic social context relates to ecological heterogeneity and dysbiosis-associated microbial shifts in school-age children remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study proposes a 'two-hit' ecological framework linking social context to shifts in the oral microbiome associated with dental caries in children. It suggests that chronic social factors create an initial ecological imbalance, which then interacts with other factors to drive dysbiosis and caries development.

papers.title: Multi-omics reveal soil microbial dysbiosis and metabolite toxicity as drivers of blueberry continuous cropping obstacles.
papers.abstract: Blueberry (Vaccinium spp.) are one of the most economically important fruit trees globally. However, due to continuous cropping have limited the industry's ability to produce consistently over the long term, and the mechanism underlying the development of this continuous cropping problem is not yet fully understood. In this study, we applied metagenomic and metabolomic to systematically detect changes in microbial community structure, function and metabolic profiles in rhizosphere and non-rhizosphere soils after different years of continuous blueberry cultivation (0, 2, 4, and 6 years) in Dali
papers.summary: This study uses metagenomics and metabolomics to investigate how continuous blueberry cultivation alters soil microbial communities and metabolite profiles, finding that microbial dysbiosis and accumulation of toxic metabolites in the rhizosphere drive the observed decline in plant productivity over years.

papers.title: A targeted PCR approach for the detection of IOLA in canine infectious respiratory disease samples during an atypical CIRD outbreak in winter 2023.
papers.abstract: During winter 2023, an atypical canine infectious respiratory disease (aCIRD) outbreak was associated with high case-fatality rates and poor antibiotic response. Preliminary metagenomics investigations claimed partial sequences resembling Infectious Organism Lurking in Human Airways (IOLA), a poorly characterized bacterium first described in humans, in canine respiratory samples. However, its detectability remained uncertain and required systematic molecular investigation.
papers.summary: This study developed a targeted PCR method to detect the bacterium IOLA in dogs during a severe respiratory disease outbreak in winter 2023, confirming its presence in canine samples. The work provides a molecular tool to investigate this poorly understood pathogen's role in canine infectious respiratory disease.

papers.title: Immunomodulatory effects of nematode-microbiota interactions in cancer therapy.
papers.abstract: Among parasites, roundworms release substances that quietly reshape how the body's defences respond - a dance shaped by long evolution. One player, called p43 - also known as Tm-DLP-1 - (Trichuris muris Dorylaimia Lipid-carrying Protein-1), makes up nearly all of it. Scientists now see this protein not just as a worm tool but as something that can steer immune responses, possibly helping fight tumours. Here lies an exploration: how p43 alters immunity, teams up with gut microbes, and shows promise in early tests and trials against cancer. Beyond its role inside the parasite hauling lipids arou
papers.summary: This paper explores how the nematode-derived protein p43 (Tm-DLP-1) modulates immune responses and interacts with gut microbiota, showing potential for cancer therapy. The protein, which constitutes a major component of roundworm secretions, can steer immune responses in ways that may help fight tumors, as demonstrated in early tests and trials.

papers.title: In-silico analysis of Bifidobacterium bifidum strain 900791 genome in the context of the B. bifidum pangenome.
papers.abstract: Bifidobacterium bifidum is a key member of the human gut microbiota with well-recognised roles in intestinal homeostasis, glycan metabolism, and immunomodulation. Strain 900791, isolated from the meconium of a Siberian infant, has been used as a commercial probiotic ingredient for decades and has demonstrated, in previously published clinical trials, an ability to improve lactose tolerance and reduce gastrointestinal symptoms in both children and adults; however, the genomic basis for these properties has not been characterised.
papers.journal: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
papers.summary: This study analyzes the genome of a commercial probiotic Bifidobacterium bifidum strain (900791) and compares it to other strains to understand its unique properties. The researchers found that while the strain shares core functions with other B. bifidum, it has specific genes related to carbohydrate metabolism and host interaction that may explain its clinical benefits for lactose tolerance and gut health.

papers.title: A scientometric analysis of research related to the 'oral-placental axis' hypothesis: current status, hotspots, and future directions.
papers.abstract: Oral diseases and placental disorders are closely associated with adverse pregnancy outcomes, and accumulating evidence supports their crosstalk that constitutes the "oral-placental axis". This study integrated scientometric and bioinformatic approaches to systematically analyze global research trends, collaboration networks, research hotspots, and core molecular-microbial mechanisms of the oral-placental axis covering the period from 2016 to 2025. A total of 196 eligible publications were retrieved from the Web of Science Core Collection, Scopus, and PubMed. Bibliometric visualization was per
papers.journal: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
papers.summary: This study uses scientometric and bioinformatic methods to analyze global research trends on the 'oral-placental axis' hypothesis, which links oral diseases to placental disorders and adverse pregnancy outcomes. Based on 196 publications from 2016-2025, it identifies key research hotspots, collaboration networks, and molecular-microbial mechanisms. The work provides a structured overview of the field's current status and future directions.

papers.title: Traditional Chinese medicine as a potential barrier-oriented sensitization strategy for immune checkpoint blockade in microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer: from resistance mechanisms to translational validation.
papers.abstract: Microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer (MSS-CRC) is refractory to immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs), mainly because of low tumor immunogenicity, impaired antigen presentation, immune exclusion, and myeloid- or Treg-dominated immunosuppression. Traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), characterized by multi-component and multi-target regulation, may provide complementary approaches for modulating these resistance barriers. This review summarizes the rationale, evidence, and translational requirements for integrating TCM with ICI-based therapy in MSS-CRC.
papers.summary: This review explores how traditional Chinese medicine (TCM) could help overcome resistance to immune checkpoint inhibitors in microsatellite-stable colorectal cancer (MSS-CRC), which is typically unresponsive to immunotherapy. The authors propose that TCM's multi-component, multi-target effects may address key resistance barriers such as low tumor immunogenicity and immune suppression. The paper summarizes the mechanistic rationale and translational considerations for combining TCM with immunotherapy in MSS-CRC.

papers.title: Investigating the Gut Microbiota-Inflammatory Cytokine-Skin Axis in Inflammatory Skin Diseases: Evidence from Mendelian Randomization.
papers.abstract: Previous studies have reported causal associations between gut microbiota and inflammatory skin diseases; however, the underlying mechanisms remain unclear. Inflammatory cytokines may mediate the gut-skin axis. This study aimed to investigate the mediating roles of inflammatory cytokines in the associations between gut microbiota and inflammatory skin diseases.
papers.summary: This study uses Mendelian randomization to explore how gut bacteria may influence inflammatory skin diseases through inflammatory cytokines. The findings suggest that certain gut microbes affect skin conditions like psoriasis and eczema by altering levels of specific inflammatory molecules. This provides evidence for a gut-skin axis mediated by the immune system.

papers.title: Shuyu Wan Potentiates PD-1 Inhibitor Efficacy in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer: Integrated Bioinformatics and Experimental Evidence for Gut Microbiota-Tumor Immune Crosstalk.
papers.abstract: Therapeutic heterogeneity limits the efficacy of immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Shuyu Wan (SYW), a classic TCM formula, has shown potential in modulating gut microbiota (GM) and enhancing immunotherapy, yet its synergistic mechanism with PD-1 inhibitors remains unclear.
papers.summary: This study shows that the traditional Chinese medicine formula Shuyu Wan (SYW) can enhance the effectiveness of PD-1 inhibitors in non-small cell lung cancer by modulating the gut microbiota and promoting anti-tumor immune responses. Using integrated bioinformatics and experimental approaches, the authors demonstrate that SYW alters gut microbial composition, which in turn improves the tumor immune microenvironment and boosts immunotherapy efficacy.

papers.title: Sparse time-varying log-ratios for longitudinal high-throughput sequencing data.
papers.abstract: High-throughput, longitudinal omics data, such as metabolomics or microbiome profiles, present analytical challenges owing to their compositional nature and irregular observation times. Although existing approaches can address compositional or temporal aspects separately, very few are tailored to capture both properties simultaneously in a high-dimensional setting. We introduce LCoDaCoRe as a supervised learning method to identify sparse time-varying log-ratio features from longitudinal, compositional data. The proposed approach integrates functional data analysis and continuous relaxation to
papers.summary: This paper introduces LCoDaCoRe, a new supervised learning method that identifies sparse, time-varying log-ratio features from longitudinal, compositional omics data (like metabolomics or microbiome profiles). It uniquely handles both the compositional nature and irregular observation times of such data by integrating functional data analysis and continuous relaxation. This provides a tool for analyzing high-dimensional, longitudinal compositional data where few existing methods address both properties simultaneously.

papers.title: Oral health status and subgingival microbiota in children with juvenile idiopathic arthritis.
papers.abstract: This study aimed to compare the oral and dental health status and the oral microbiota of patients with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) with those of healthy children.
papers.journal: Frontiers in cellular and infection microbiology
papers.summary: This study compared oral health and subgingival microbiota between children with juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) and healthy controls, finding differences in microbial composition associated with the autoimmune condition. The results suggest that JIA may influence the oral microbiome, potentially impacting dental health management in affected children.

papers.title: Improving DirectLiNGAM for high-dimensional microbiome data: roots screening and eBIC based model selection.
papers.abstract: Identifying causal relationships from observational data is a central challenge in gut microbiome research, where complex, multivariate interactions shape host health and disease. These data are typically high-dimensional and sample-limited, creating substantial obstacles for causal discovery and motivating the development of methods tailored to this regime. In this study, we address this challenge by focusing on DirectLiNGAM and introducing two complementary methodological improvements designed to facilitate its practical application in microbiome data. Specifically, we propose two extensions
papers.summary: This study improves the DirectLiNGAM causal discovery method to better handle high-dimensional, sample-limited microbiome data. The authors introduce two extensions—roots screening and eBIC-based model selection—that make the method more practical for identifying causal relationships in complex microbial communities.

papers.title: The Plastic Within: Micro- and Nanoplastics in Human Tissues and the Nutritional Context for Exposure Mitigation.
papers.abstract: Microplastics (MPs) and nanoplastics (NPs) are increasingly detected in human tissues, prompting concern about potential biological effects. Yet, for most outcomes, the literature remains dominated by detection studies and preclinical toxicology, with limited human dose-response data.
papers.summary: This review highlights the growing detection of micro- and nanoplastics in human tissues and discusses nutritional strategies that might mitigate exposure. The authors note that most current evidence comes from detection studies and animal models, with little human dose-response data available.
